food_name: Brown Sugar Fresh Milk with Tapioca Pearls

local_names.malay: Susu Segar Gula Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka

aliases: Brown Sugar Fresh Milk with Tapioca Pearls
Susu Segar Gula Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka

malay: Susu Segar Gula Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka

cuisine: Taiwanese origin; ubiquitous in Singapore bubble tea chains
region_of_origin: Invented by Tiger Sugar (Taiwan) circa 2018; rapidly adopted by Singapore chains including LiHo, Gong Cha, and others
food_subgroup: Sweetened milk beverage with tapioca pearls; brown sugar syrup and fresh whole milk with black tapioca pearls — not tea-based

ontology.foodon_label: milk-based beverage (closest parent — sweetened milk beverage with starch inclusion)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for brown sugar boba milk. FOODON_03301710 (milk-based beverage) is the closest defensible parent. No exact match exists for the brown sugar syrup and tapioca pearl composite.
ontology.foodex2_description: Milk-based beverages (closest parent in FoodEx2 hierarchy; no specific code exists for brown sugar tapioca milk drink)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A003K covers milk-based beverages as the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for brown sugar boba milk. This is a novel composite beverage category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to brown sugar boba milk as a food item. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not cover this composite beverage.

serving.description: 1 cup = 500 ml, approximately 520 g (500 ml liquid including brown sugar syrup and fresh milk, plus approximately 50 g cooked tapioca pearls; ice displaces some liquid volume)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one cup of 500 ml (approximately 520 g), comprising fresh whole milk, brown sugar syrup drizzled inside the cup, and approximately 50 g of cooked black tapioca pearls, served with ice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA SR Legacy: Whole milk (FDC ID 1097512); Sugars, brown (FDC ID 1100869); Tapioca, pearl, dry (FDC ID 169717) — composite estimate weighted by recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — SR Legacy via FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this composite beverage. Values are derived by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for whole milk (~350 ml, ~360 g), brown sugar syrup (~38 g added sugar equivalent), and cooked tapioca pearls (~50 g). Proportions reflect a standard 500 ml Tiger Sugar / LiHo-style serving. Per-100g values are back-calculated from the 520 g composite. Energy cross-checked against publicly disclosed nutrition panels from LiHo Singapore (approximately 490–510 kcal per 500 ml cup), consistent with the derived values.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous components: brown sugar / sucrose GI ~65; cooked white tapioca pearls GI ~94 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008); composite weighted estimate for brown sugar boba milk drink ~85
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite beverage. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for the dominant carbohydrate contributors: (1) brown sugar / sucrose GI ~65 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) cooked tapioca pearls GI ~94 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283); (3) fresh whole milk GI ~27 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite GI ~85 reflects the high tapioca starch load (~50 g cooked pearls per serving) and concentrated brown sugar syrup. Glycemic load per 520 g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate in grams per serving) / 100 = (85 × 82) / 100 ≈ 70.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content driving metabolic risk; low direct sodium
hypertension.guidance: Direct sodium content is low at 38 mg per 100 g (approximately 198 mg per 500 ml serving), posing no direct sodium-related hypertension risk; however, the very high added sugar load of approximately 38–55 g per cup contributes to weight gain, insulin resistance, and metabolic dysfunction — all established indirect risk factors for hypertension progression — and regular consumption should be discouraged in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 85, approximately 82 g total carbohydrate per serving, glycemic load ~70
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 500 ml cup delivers approximately 82 g total carbohydrate (approximately 50 g added brown sugar syrup plus approximately 32 g tapioca starch) at a very high glycemic index of ~85 and glycemic load of ~70 per serving — far exceeding the recommended single-meal carbohydrate target of 45–60 g for most adults with type 2 diabetes — and patients must be counselled that brown sugar is not a safer alternative to white sugar, as both have a GI of approximately 65 and are metabolically equivalent.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar load; saturated fat 1.5 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is moderate at 1.5 g per 100 g (approximately 7.8 g per serving), but the primary cardiovascular concern is the very high added sugar content of approximately 38–55 g per cup, which is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular disease risk with habitual consumption; this drink should be classified as an occasional treat rather than a regular beverage for patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Fresh Milk with Tapioca Pearls (黑糖鲜奶珍珠 / Hēitáng Xiānnǎi Zhēnzhū) is the highest-selling and most visually distinctive bubble tea variant in Singapore since its introduction by Tiger Sugar (台湾虎记茶) from Taiwan circa 2018. The drink is defined by its iconic 'tiger stripe' presentation: thick, concentrated brown sugar caramel syrup is swirled dramatically up the inside walls of a clear plastic cup before fresh whole milk is poured in, creating brown-and-white stripe patterns. Black tapioca pearls, slow-cooked in brown sugar syrup until deeply caramelised and glossy, are added to complete the drink. Critically, this beverage contains NO brewed tea — it is composed entirely of fresh whole milk, brown sugar syrup, and tapioca pearls — making it unique among boba variants and clinically distinct: it provides no caffeine, no tea polyphenols, and no antioxidant benefit associated with tea-based boba. A standard 500 ml serving (approximately 520 g) delivers approximately 494 kcal, 82 g total carbohydrate, and 38–55 g total sugar — approaching or exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 50 g free sugars in a single drink. The glycemic index is approximately 85 (high) and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 70, driven by the combination of brown sugar syrup and high-GI cooked tapioca starch. A critical patient misconception must be proactively addressed: brown sugar is NOT healthier than white sugar — both have a GI of approximately 65 and are metabolically equivalent. The tiger stripe visual is purely aesthetic. This drink is contraindicated for patients with type 2 diabetes and should be treated as an occasional indulgence by all others. Per 100 g: energy 95 kcal, protein 2.8 g, fat 2.5 g, carbohydrate 15.8 g, sugar 10.2 g, sodium 38 mg.
common_user_queries: brown sugar vs white sugar healthier

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct entry for this composite beverage. Values are derived by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for whole milk, brown sugar, and cooked tapioca pearls, cross-checked against publicly disclosed LiHo Singapore nutrition panels. Confidence is medium because the composite derivation introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients and the exact sugar contribution from the brown sugar syrup drizzle, which varies by chain and customisation level.
notes: Serving size reflects a standard 500 ml cup as sold at Tiger Sugar and LiHo Singapore at full sugar level. Sugar content varies significantly with customisation (less sugar options reduce added sugar by approximately 25–75%). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are reported as -1 (not available) because USDA SR Legacy does not provide these values for the cooked tapioca pearl component and the composite calculation cannot reliably disaggregate them; this is documented per G3 numeric hygiene rules. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement of this specific composite drink has been published in a peer-reviewed journal as of the accessed date.
